hook: You already know how to solve equations and work with variables — now it's time to *see* the math come to life. Numbers alone can't always show you the full picture, but a graph can instantly reveal patterns, trends, and relationships that equations hide. What if you could look at a straight line and immediately know its speed, direction, and starting point?
concept: This unit is about connecting equations to pictures: every linear equation tells a story, and its graph is the visual version of that story. The unifying idea is the *linear function* — a relationship where a constant rate of change produces a perfectly straight line.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Coordinate Plane and Plotting Points
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to navigate the coordinate plane by precisely locating and plotting ordered pairs $(x, y)$ — the foundation for everything you'll graph this unit.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Introduction to Functions and Function Notation
topic_roadmap.description: Master the concept of a function as a reliable input-output rule, and learn to read and write function notation like $f(x)$ with confidence.
You will learn to identify the set of allowable inputs (domain) and corresponding outputs (range) for a function, building your ability to describe what a function can and cannot do.
Apply a systematic table-of-values approach to graph any linear equation by plotting multiple points and connecting them into a straight line.
You will learn to calculate slope using $m =  rac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$ and interpret it as a rate of change that describes how steeply and in which direction a line rises or falls.
Master graphing lines quickly and efficiently by using the slope $m$ and $y$-intercept $b$ directly from the equation $y = mx + b$.
You will learn to graph a linear equation by finding only two key points — where the line crosses the $x$- and $y$-axes — making graphing faster for certain equation forms.
Discover why horizontal lines have a slope of zero and vertical lines have undefined slope, and learn to instantly graph equations like $y = c$ and $x = c$.
